module.m2.title: Sustainability Management in Aquaculture
topic.m2.t1.title: Environmental, social and economic issues linked to shellfish and finfish farming
q.m2.t1.001: Aquaculture refers to the cultivation of aquatic organisms. Which of the following is not one of the aims of aquaculture?
q.m2.t1.001.b: To provide sustainable access to animal protein.
q.m2.t1.002: Which of the following is not a species cultivated in marine aquaculture?
q.m2.t1.003: How are the effects of aquaculture practices not shaped on the environment in which they are located?
q.m2.t1.004: The rapid development process in aquaculture after the 1980s has not directly caused changes in which area?
q.m2.t1.004.a: Reduction of labor force with remote sensing systems.
q.m2.t1.004.b: Structural and numerical development of processing and evaluation factories,
q.m2.t1.004.c: More discussion of the pressures of intensive farming practices on the environment.
q.m2.t1.004.d: Emphasis on training technical personnel to prevent economic losses and increase farm profitability.
q.m2.t1.005: Which of the following is not a common component in commercial feeds used in fish farming?
q.m2.t1.006: Which of the following applications does not directly contribute to increasing productivity in aquaculture?
q.m2.t1.006.a: Treating farm effluent and releasing it back into nature
q.m2.t1.006.d: Biotechnological applications such as triplodization, all female-all male stock production
q.m2.t1.007: Which of the following cannot be said for Recirculating Aquaculture Systems (RAS)?
q.m2.t1.007.a: RAS systems planned for freshwater fish are not suitable for marine fish.
q.m2.t1.007.b: They are costly systems in terms of installation and operation.
q.m2.t1.007.c: Production can be carried out in every season of the year.
q.m2.t1.007.d: Since very low flow rates are required, they are systems that can be built almost anywhere.
q.m2.t1.008: Which of the following is not true about the effects of climate change on industrial aquaculture?
q.m2.t1.008.a: It causes negative effects on the marketing quality of farmed fish.
q.m2.t1.008.b: Global warming is a threat because it causes water flow rates to warm.
q.m2.t1.008.c: Climate change affects the lowest and highest temperatures of water resources.
q.m2.t1.008.d: Since erosion increases, the increase in the suspension load of water poses a serious threat.
q.m2.t1.009: Which of the following cannot be said in terms of the direct contributions of universities and R&D to aquaculture development?
q.m2.t1.009.b: Preparing competent technical personnel for the sector,
q.m2.t1.009.c: Determining pathogens that may cause economic loss and determining their treatments,
q.m2.t1.009.d: Determining the aquaculture performances of alternative species,
q.m2.t1.010: Which of the following is incorrect in the context of the relationship between aquaculture and the environment?
q.m2.t1.010.a: The use of mechanization in site selection and project design in aquaculture completely prevents possible environmental problems.
q.m2.t1.010.b: The adequacy of consumer fish demand from aquaculture can reduce fishing pressure on natural fish stocks.
q.m2.t1.010.c: Uncontrolled waste release can cause pollution in the aquatic ecosystem.
q.m2.t1.010.d: The escape of individuals of the species used in aquaculture from the farm into nature can cause some ecological pressures.
q.m2.t1.011: The term Inshore Shellfish Aquaculture can be fully explained by which of the following?
q.m2.t1.011.a: Shellfish culture is done on rafts, baskets or stakes in the intertidal zone in the coastal area, on suspension ropes in relatively deep waters.
q.m2.t1.011.c: Releasing mussels and oysters in the sea area close to the shore.
q.m2.t1.012: Which of the following is not a shellfish species cultivated?
q.m2.t1.013: Which of the following is not correct for shellfish culture?
q.m2.t1.013.a: It is quite easy and common to grow with commercial feeds produced specifically for the species.
q.m2.t1.013.b: They feed on planktonic organisms and organic particles in the water by filtration,
q.m2.t1.013.c: While mussels and oysters live sessile (attached to a substrate), scallops do not,
q.m2.t1.013.d: They are grown in large quantities in Europe, the USA and China.
q.m2.t1.014: Which of the following is not one of the main reasons for the spread of shellfish farming?
q.m2.t1.014.a: The grown product can be marketed without the need for a processing factory.
q.m2.t1.014.b: The establishment of shellfish farms is easier and less costly than the establishment of finfish farms.
q.m2.t1.014.c: The absence of the need for feed use in shellfish farming reduces operating costs.
q.m2.t1.014.d: It is an important advantage that planned farming improves the water quality in the region where it is grown.
q.m2.t1.015: For which living group is shellfish farming done?
q.m2.t1.016: Which of the following is a species grown in a suspension system?
q.m2.t1.017: For which species are tray and basket systems generally used?
q.m2.t1.018: How do shellfish contribute to the environment?
q.m2.t1.018.a: Removes suspended load from water by filtration
q.m2.t1.018.d: Starts the process of nitrification and denitrification
q.m2.t1.019: What does the term “carrying capacity” refer to?
q.m2.t1.019.a: Maximum production pressure that an ecosystem can tolerate
q.m2.t1.019.c: Farm pool number to project capacity ratio
q.m2.t1.020: What can shellfish feces and shell residues cause?
q.m2.t1.020.a: Organic-inorganic load accumulation on the ground
q.m2.t1.020.b: Foam formation on the water surface
q.m2.t1.021: Which of the following should be applied to reduce the risk of disease in culture systems?
q.m2.t1.022: Why is cultivation preferred in coastal areas?
q.m2.t1.022.a: Because it is protected in terms of mechanical effects compared to the open sea
q.m2.t1.022.b: Due to the ease of fresh water
q.m2.t1.022.c: Because of its low economic value
q.m2.t1.022.d: Because of the lower risk of disease
q.m2.t1.023: How does acidification affect shellfish species?
q.m2.t1.024: What is the aim of Integrated Multitrophic Aquaculture (IMTA) systems?
q.m2.t1.024.a: Cultivation of species with different nutritional characteristics in the same habitat
q.m2.t1.024.b: Intensive production with a single species
q.m2.t1.025: Shellfish farms operating in coastal areas may conflict with which sector due to visual pollution?
q.m2.t1.026: Which of the following is a common location for inshore aquaculture?
q.m2.t1.026.a: Inshore coastal areas, bays and lagoons
q.m2.t1.026.c: Deep sea areas with water depths greater than 70 m
q.m2.t1.027: Which species are commonly grown in inshore marine farming in Europe?
q.m2.t1.028: Which type of pollution occurs in aquaculture farms due to the mechanical breakdown of plastic materials such as nets and ropes?
q.m2.t1.029: In near-shore farms, what environmental impact does feed residue, and feces accumulate on the seabed?
q.m2.t1.029.a: Anoxic sediment structure and hydrogen sulphide production
q.m2.t1.030: What effects does hydrogen sulphide (H₂S) production have on the seabed?
q.m2.t1.031: What kind of risk does the overuse of antibiotics increase in near-shore systems?
q.m2.t1.031.a: Development of bacteria resistant to antibiotics
q.m2.t1.032: How do marine mammals interact in coastal farms?
q.m2.t1.032.a: They damage cage nets in farms, causing fish escape and thus negative interaction with the natural environment.
q.m2.t1.032.b: Aquatic mammals stay away from areas where systems are installed
q.m2.t1.033: Which predators can destroy nets in coastal farms?
q.m2.t1.034: What is the main factor causing social conflict in coastal systems?
q.m2.t1.035: What effects can farm fish escaping into the wild cause?
q.m2.t1.035.a: Genetic pollution, predation pressure and feeding competition
q.m2.t1.036: Which of the following options is not one of the main ingredients in fish food?
q.m2.t1.037.a: Amount of feed needed to achieve unit fish weight gain
q.m2.t1.038: What is the investment advantage of near-shore systems?
q.m2.t1.038.a: Relatively low installation and logistics costs
q.m2.t1.039: What situation can cause ecological risks in near-shore farms?
q.m2.t1.039.a: Breakage of nets and escape of fish
q.m2.t1.040: Which method is recommended to reduce the environmental impact of near-shore aquaculture?
q.m2.t1.040.a: Designing Multitrophic Aquaculture System (IMTA) in farm planning
q.m2.t1.041: Which issue is more likely to cause conflict with urban living communities in farms close to the coast?
q.m2.t1.041.a: Visual pollution, tourism concerns and environmental impacts
q.m2.t1.042: Which of the following is an advantage of offshore farming?
q.m2.t1.042.a: Easier dispersion of waste and less pressure on the environment
q.m2.t1.043: What is the disadvantage of offshore systems?
q.m2.t1.044: Which structures are not common in offshore farms?
q.m2.t1.044.a: Use of fish finder echo sounders
q.m2.t1.045: Why is it more difficult for fish diseases to spread in the open sea?
q.m2.t1.046: What is the biggest problem in terms of tourism and social acceptance in coastal systems?
q.m2.t1.046.a: Visual pollution and land use conflicts
q.m2.t1.047: What can organic waste accumulated at the bottom in coastal systems lead to?
q.m2.t1.048: Which area is more advantageous for offshore systems than coastal?
q.m2.t1.048.a: Acceptability of fish meat quality and growth rate
q.m2.t1.049: What is the main reason why diseases cause more economic loss in cages close to the shore?
q.m2.t1.049.c: Cages are in the open sea
q.m2.t1.050: Which of the following is necessary for offshore farms?
q.m2.t1.050.d: Data transmission systems with online monitoring systems
q.m2.t1.051: In which system is fish more likely to escape to nature?
q.m2.t1.052: Which system is associated with high-tech automation and drone investments?
q.m2.t1.053: What do regulations generally target in offshore farming?
q.m2.t1.053.d: Providing training for fish farm workers.
q.m2.t1.054: Which of the following can increase sustainability in offshore farming?
q.m2.t1.055: Why can offshore areas act as a buffer against climate change?
q.m2.t1.055.a: Provide more stable environmental conditions than coastal areas
q.m2.t1.055.c: Prevent conflict with tourism and urban life
q.m2.t1.056: Which of the following is the social impact of the offshore system?
q.m2.t1.056.a: Potential for negative interactions and conflicts with society and industry stakeholders
q.m2.t1.057: What is one of the reasons for the slow development of offshore production?
q.m2.t1.058: Which of the following is the investment advantage of offshore systems?
q.m2.t1.058.a: High capacity, predictable and repeatable production
q.m2.t1.058.b: Providing profit in a short time
q.m2.t1.059: Which of the following is one of the ecological risks of near-shore systems?
q.m2.t1.060: What method is recommended to make near-shore systems more environmentally friendly?
q.m2.t1.060.a: Use of Integrated Multitrophic Aquaculture (IMTA) systems
q.m2.t1.061: Which of the following is not an advantage of RAS systems over FTS?
q.m2.t1.061.d: Prevention of fish escape into nature
q.m2.t1.062: What is the reuse rate of used water in RAS systems?
q.m2.t1.063: What is the factor that most limits the spread of RAS systems?
q.m2.t1.064: Which of the following is not a component of the RAS system?
q.m2.t1.065: Which of the following is not a significantly less common environmental risk in RAS systems compared to flowthrough systems?
q.m2.t1.065.b: Transmission of pathogens to natural populations
q.m2.t1.065.d: Release of organic and inorganic substances
q.m2.t1.066: What is the direct effect of untreated wastewater released in flowthrough systems on the ecosystem?
q.m2.t1.067: What contributes to the improvement of Feed Conversion Ratio (FCR) in RAS systems?
q.m2.t1.068: What does the use of renewable energy in RAS systems provide?
q.m2.t1.068.d: Reduces the lethal effects of nitrite
q.m2.t1.069: Which of the following options is more variable in flowthrough systems?
q.m2.t1.069.a: All the above options are correct.
q.m2.t1.070: What is one of the social advantages of RAS systems?
q.m2.t1.070.a: Does not create bad odors and visual pollution
q.m2.t1.071: Which of the following is not a typical feature of RAS systems?
q.m2.t1.071.a: High water flow rate is needed
q.m2.t1.071.d: Photoperiod can be adjusted according to business profitability
q.m2.t1.072: What difficulties does climate change bring with it with FTS systems?
q.m2.t1.072.a: Effects of drought, precipitation and turbidity are constantly becoming more visible
q.m2.t1.072.b: Increased cost due to energy fluctuations
q.m2.t1.072.d: Damage to fish gills because of excessive oxygenation
q.m2.t1.073: Which of the following is not valid for RAS installation?
q.m2.t1.073.a: Installation and operation are very costly except in places where wind energy is possible.
q.m2.t1.073.b: The presence of trained personnel in RAS installation and operation is important for sustainability.
q.m2.t1.073.c: The elimination of the lethal effects of carbon dioxide and ammonia has not yet been fully resolved.
q.m2.t1.073.d: Economic loss may be high due to damage to the entire system in the event of disease transmission.
q.m2.t1.074: Which of the features to be considered in site selection for RAS and FTS installation is given incorrectly?
q.m2.t1.074.a: RAS can be recommended in cold climates, FYS can be recommended in temperate climates
q.m2.t1.074.b: RAS installation should be preferred in biologically fragile areas
q.m2.t1.074.c: FTS installation with a capacity suitable for water flow can be recommended in places where clean water can be supplied
q.m2.t1.074.d: RAS can be installed in areas where urban life is dense
q.m2.t1.075: In which system is the risk of fish escaping less?
q.m2.t1.075.c: In net cage systems in dams
q.m2.t1.075.d: In net cage systems in the sea
q.m2.t1.076: Which of the following ingredients can play a role in reducing pressure on marine ecosystems?
q.m2.t1.077: Which of the following increases as temperature increases?
q.m2.t1.078: What is the factor that makes disease control difficult in flowthrough systems?
q.m2.t1.078.a: Effect of pathogens that may come from the water source
q.m2.t1.079: Which of the following factors cannot have a positive effect on economic sustainability in RAS systems?
q.m2.t1.079.a: Keeping fish meat quality high and increasing market value
q.m2.t1.079.b: Integrated production planning with FTS systems
q.m2.t1.079.c: Planning water temperature and light to ensure fish welfare at the best conditions
q.m2.t1.079.d: Reducing the cost per unit of fish production by keeping FCR low
q.m2.t1.080: Which method is promising for the combined use of both systems for the future?
q.m2.t1.080.a: Performing the fry period in RAS and transferring them to FTS after the fry stage
q.m2.t1.080.b: Conducting separate breeding activities in both systems
q.m2.t1.080.c: Performing incubation in RAS, early-stage fry feeding in FTS
q.m2.t1.080.d: Placing sick fish in RAS and transferring recovered fish to FTS
topic.m2.t2.title: Good practices for a sustainable management of shellfish and finfish farming
q.m2.t2.001.a: A set of methods or techniques that has been proven to reliably lead to desired outcomes, efficiency, and quality
q.m2.t2.001.b: Correct behaviours that maximize sustainability in industry
q.m2.t2.001.c: Avoiding employee training to save time and resources
q.m2.t2.001.d: Maximizing short-term profits regardless of environmental and social impacts
q.m2.t2.002: What is the primary goal of aquaculture certification schemes like ASC or Best Aquaculture Practices (BAP)?
q.m2.t2.002.a: To ensure responsible and sustainable aquaculture practices
q.m2.t2.002.b: To increase the global seafood trade regardless of sustainability
q.m2.t2.002.c: To maximize fish production through genetic modification
q.m2.t2.002.d: To promote the use of synthetic feeds in aquaculture
q.m2.t2.003: Do good practices oblige companies to follow certain rules and methods?
q.m2.t2.004: Why is adopting good practices useful?
q.m2.t2.004.a: It improves efficiency and sustainability, offering rapid integration of new approaches
q.m2.t2.004.b: It helps companies bypass regulations and reduce operational costs
q.m2.t2.004.d: It focuses primarily on meeting deadlines rather than process improvement
q.m2.t2.005: Which of the following is a key reason why adopting good practices is important in aquaculture?
q.m2.t2.005.a: To enhance efficiency, sustainability, and social acceptability while promoting innovation
q.m2.t2.005.b: To maximize short-term profits regardless of environmental impact
q.m2.t2.005.c: To improve compliance with regulatory requirements and simplify operations
q.m2.t2.005.d: To ensure aquaculture practices integrate with local communities and stakeholders
q.m2.t2.006.a: Benefits people and societies obtain from natural ecosystems
q.m2.t2.006.b: Scientific methods used to study and protect biodiversity
q.m2.t2.006.c: Government programs that regulate multiple industry sectors
q.m2.t2.006.d: Technological services used in aquaculture farms
q.m2.t2.007: Which of the following best represents a provisioning ecosystem service?
q.m2.t2.008: What is an example of a regulating service provided by ecosystems?
q.m2.t2.008.a: Natural pollination of crops by bees
q.m2.t2.008.b: Production of raw materials for clothing
q.m2.t2.008.c: Opportunities for hiking in national parks
q.m2.t2.009: Which of the following is considered a supporting ecosystem service?
q.m2.t2.010: Which of the following illustrates a cultural ecosystem service?
q.m2.t2.010.a: Recreational use of a forest for hiking
q.m2.t2.011: Why are good practices important in the aquaculture industry?
q.m2.t2.011.a: They help aquaculture businesses streamline processes, reduce waste, and optimise resources
q.m2.t2.011.b: They eliminate the need for regulation and external oversight
q.m2.t2.011.c: They ensure fish grow faster regardless of environmental conditions
q.m2.t2.011.d: They increase the use of synthetic chemicals to enhance yields
q.m2.t2.012: How can aquaculture operations improve their social acceptability?
q.m2.t2.012.a: By engaging local communities and following transparent, ethical practices
q.m2.t2.012.b: By avoiding any interaction with local stakeholders
q.m2.t2.012.c: By focusing solely on profitability, ignoring environmental or social concerns
q.m2.t2.012.d: By releasing untreated waste into surrounding ecosystems to reduce costs
q.m2.t2.013: The Doñana marshes in Southern Spain are used as an example of aquaculture practices focused solely on fish production, with little emphasis on environmental services or recreation
q.m2.t2.014: What is one recommended practice for managing hazardous materials on a shellfish farm?
q.m2.t2.014.a: Label and store chemicals according to legal requirements
q.m2.t2.014.b: Store chemicals outdoors in uncovered containers
q.m2.t2.014.c: Burn flammable materials to reduce volume
q.m2.t2.014.d: Mix lubricants with seawater to dilute their impact
q.m2.t2.015: How can farms reduce negative impacts on habitats and biodiversity?
q.m2.t2.015.c: Regularly dredge sediment to keep the seabed level
q.m2.t2.015.d: Use loud noises to keep away local wildlife
q.m2.t2.016: Which of the following is a good biosecurity practice for disease prevention?
q.m2.t2.016.a: Regularly monitor stock health and remove dead individuals
q.m2.t2.016.b: Transport sick animals to nearby farms for observation
q.m2.t2.016.c: Use uncertified seed stock to reduce costs
q.m2.t2.016.d: Ignore minor mortalities unless they exceed 50%
q.m2.t2.017: What should farms do to help manage adverse sea and weather conditions?
q.m2.t2.017.a: Use anchoring systems of suitable design and weight
q.m2.t2.017.b: Use oversized ropes to lift the entire farm out of the water during storms
q.m2.t2.017.c: Ignore mooring lines unless they completely break
q.m2.t2.017.d: Avoid replacing degraded materials unless inspected by authorities
q.m2.t2.018: What is recommended for reusing shellfish processing waste?
q.m2.t2.018.a: Valorize byproducts like oyster shells in local economies
q.m2.t2.018.b: Export all processing waste to international markets
q.m2.t2.018.d: Discard oyster shells in landfills as standard practice
q.m2.t2.019: How can biotoxin risks be reduced in shellfish production?
q.m2.t2.019.a: Establish communication with stakeholders and develop alert systems
q.m2.t2.019.b: Keep shellfish into open waters during contamination
q.m2.t2.019.c: Cancel health surveillance networks to reduce costs
q.m2.t2.019.d: Store contaminated batches for human consumption later
q.m2.t2.020: Which of the following supports good community relations in shellfish farming?
q.m2.t2.020.a: Promote guided visits and promote educational activities
q.m2.t2.020.b: Keep operations secret to prevent local interference
q.m2.t2.020.c: Avoid sharing farming operations to the public
q.m2.t2.020.d: Limit communication to government officials only
q.m2.t2.021: It is good practice to minimise the use of bleach and instead use biodegradable cleaning products in shellfish farms
q.m2.t2.022: Shellfish farms should avoid informing local communities about their operations to maintain competitive advantage
q.m2.t2.023: Farmers must report disease outbreaks to authorities and inform neighbouring producers
q.m2.t2.024: Eliminating all predators is required to protect farmed shellfish
q.m2.t2.025: In finfish aquaculture, what are examples of biosecurity measures to mitigate the risks of contracting and spreading diseases?
q.m2.t2.025.a: Proper management of dead animals, rodent control programmes when needed and disinfection of equipment and tanks
q.m2.t2.025.b: Monitoring the spread of invasive species, training programmes for operators and replace degraded materials
q.m2.t2.025.c: Keeping production area organized and disposal of waste through authorized companies
q.m2.t2.025.d: Using wild-caught fish exclusively and continuous water quality monitoring
q.m2.t2.026: What is an effective solution for managing farm structures in adverse weather?
q.m2.t2.026.a: Adopt a rigorous maintenance schedule for all farm components, promptly replacing any degraded materials
q.m2.t2.026.b: Utilize certified materials and maintain tight spacing between structures to enhance resilience against adverse weather conditions
q.m2.t2.026.c: Minimize anchoring systems to optimize flexibility
q.m2.t2.026.d: Weight ropes and lines with heavy structures to improve stability and resistance
q.m2.t2.027: Which of the following are recommended good practices for managing waste in aquaculture operations?
q.m2.t2.027.a: Develop a waste management plan, minimize waste generation, and ensure proper recycling or disposal at designated sites
q.m2.t2.027.b: Use hazardous chemicals only when absolutely necessary, even if labeling requirements are not met
q.m2.t2.027.c: Dispose of waste, including chemicals, in nearby areas where it can naturally decompose over time
q.m2.t2.027.d: Focus primarily on reducing visible waste while postponing appropriate recycling whenever possible
q.m2.t2.028: What is one good practice to prevent contamination from residues and zoonotic agents?
q.m2.t2.028.a: Maintain separate storage systems and ensure traceability
q.m2.t2.028.b: Avoid cleaning equipment too often to conserve water
q.m2.t2.028.c: Use expired fish feed if it still smells fine
q.m2.t2.028.d: Store disinfectants and feed together for easier access
q.m2.t2.029: Structures, equipment, and vehicles on aquaculture farms must comply with both environmental and worker safety regulations
q.m2.t2.030: To avoid disturbing neighbors, farms should increase lighting and allow material piles to accumulate near residential areas
topic.m2.t3.title: Integrated Multi-trophic Aquaculture technique in shellfish farming
q.m2.t3.001: What is one of the main ecological benefits of shellfish aquaculture?
q.m2.t3.001.a: Enhanced nitrogen and phosphorus absorption from coastal waters
q.m2.t3.001.d: Warming of surface water for faster breeding
q.m2.t3.002: Which of the following contributes to the habitat complexity of shellfish farms?
q.m2.t3.002.a: Installation of poles and lines that create complex structures
q.m2.t3.002.b: Use of large nets to block predators
q.m2.t3.002.c: Constant removal of shell detritus to keep areas clean
q.m2.t3.003: How can shellfish aquaculture help address coastal eutrophication?
q.m2.t3.003.a: By removing excess nutrients like nitrogen and phosphorus
q.m2.t3.003.c: By releasing oxygen into the water
q.m2.t3.003.d: By preventing wave action near shores
q.m2.t3.004: What was one notable ecological outcome of offshore mussel farming in Lyme Bay (UK)?
q.m2.t3.004.a: Enrichment and diversification of seabed habitats
q.m2.t3.004.b: Decrease in fish and invertebrate diversity
q.m2.t3.005: Shellfish aquaculture can provide structured habitats that are functionally similar to natural reefs
q.m2.t3.006: Shellfish aquaculture, by providing additional food resources and structured habitats, may contribute to greater wild species abundance—even though some effects could reflect aggregation rather than true biomass increases
q.m2.t3.007: What is the primary difference between off-bottom and on-bottom bivalve culture?
q.m2.t3.007.a: Off-bottom culture suspends bivalves above the seafloor; on-bottom culture farms them directly on or in it
q.m2.t3.007.b: Off-bottom culture is only used in freshwater; on-bottom in saltwater
q.m2.t3.007.c: On-bottom culture always requires artificial cages; off-bottom does not
q.m2.t3.007.d: Off-bottom culture uses only hatchery-raised seed; on-bottom uses wild seed only
q.m2.t3.008: Which of the following is a clear ecological benefit of shellfish farms
q.m2.t3.008.b: Decreasing oxygen levels through bivalve excretion
q.m2.t3.008.c: Permanently removing native invasive fish species
q.m2.t3.008.d: Increasing carbon dioxide in the water
q.m2.t3.009: Why might shellfish farms serve as nursery areas for fish?
q.m2.t3.009.a: Because artificial structures offer shelter and food
q.m2.t3.009.b: Because fish are fed pellets at the shellfish farms
q.m2.t3.009.c: Due to the enhanced lighting used at night
q.m2.t3.009.d: Because they use electric barriers to keep out predators
q.m2.t3.010: How do mussel farms can contribute to wild fish populations?
q.m2.t3.010.a: They provide food and structural habitat that support juvenile fish
q.m2.t3.010.c: They fertilize the water with chemicals
q.m2.t3.010.d: They introduce new fish species artificially
q.m2.t3.011: Shellfish farms can act as Fish Aggregating Devices (FADs), but also offer additional benefits like food supply and shelter that can enhance biodiversity
q.m2.t3.012: Suspended mussel farms can only benefit the environment by increasing bivalve density but do not influence fish communities
q.m2.t3.013: What are Fish Aggregating Devices (FADs) in the context of marine ecosystems?
q.m2.t3.013.a: Structures that attract and concentrate fish by providing shelter or food
q.m2.t3.013.b: Devices used to keep potential predators away from aquaculture sites
q.m2.t3.013.c: Tools for measuring water salinity in fish farms
q.m2.t3.013.d: Floating GPS trackers used to monitor fish migration
q.m2.t3.014: What is a key difference between wild and hatchery-based seed sourcing in bivalve aquaculture?
q.m2.t3.014.a: Wild seed is collected from natural spawning events, while hatchery seed is produced under controlled conditions
q.m2.t3.014.b: Hatchery seed always produces more performing bivalves than wild seed
q.m2.t3.014.c: Wild seed can only be used for off-bottom culture, not on-bottom culture
q.m2.t3.014.d: Hatchery seed is illegal to use in most coastal aquaculture operations
q.m2.t3.015: What is a key difference between co-culture and IMTA in shellfish farming?
q.m2.t3.015.a: IMTA integrates species across trophic levels and typically includes fish farming
q.m2.t3.015.b: Co-culture excludes macroalgae, while IMTA includes them
q.m2.t3.015.c: IMTA involves species at the same trophic level, while co-culture uses different levels
q.m2.t3.015.d: Co-culture requires saltwater ponds, while IMTA operates in open water
q.m2.t3.016: What is the primary objective of IMTA?
q.m2.t3.016.a: Minimizing environmental impact and improving efficiency through waste recycling
q.m2.t3.016.b: Maximizing fish production at the expense of other species
q.m2.t3.016.c: Eliminating the need for natural currents in aquaculture systems
q.m2.t3.016.d: Focusing solely on bivalve growth rates
q.m2.t3.017: How do bivalves contribute to nutrient removal in co-culture systems?
q.m2.t3.017.a: Filtering particulate organic matter (e.g., phytoplankton) from the water
q.m2.t3.017.b: By absorbing dissolved inorganic nutrients like ammonium and phosphate
q.m2.t3.017.c: By increasing seawater pH through photosynthesis
q.m2.t3.017.d: Producing oxygen that enhances macroalgae growth
q.m2.t3.018: What role do macroalgae play in co-culture with shellfish?
q.m2.t3.018.a: Absorbing dissolved inorganic nutrients (e.g., nitrate) from the water
q.m2.t3.018.b: Providing direct food for bivalves like oysters and mussels
q.m2.t3.018.c: Reducing turbidity by consuming fish metabolic waste
q.m2.t3.018.d: Increasing fish farming efficiency in IMTA systems
q.m2.t3.019: Why has IMTA shifted toward shellfish-macroalgae co-culture?
q.m2.t3.019.a: Because of higher costs, uncertain benefits, and regulatory challenges in traditional IMTA
q.m2.t3.019.b: Due to simplified regulatory requirements for single-species systems
q.m2.t3.019.c: Macroalgae cannot survive in IMTA systems with fish
q.m2.t3.019.d: Bivalves outperform fish in nutrient recycling efficiency
q.m2.t3.020: In the Charentes region co-culture, how do imperial shrimp thrive?
q.m2.t3.020.a: Through natural consumption of live prey in oyster beds
q.m2.t3.020.b: By feeding on waste directly from oyster metabolism
q.m2.t3.020.c: Relying on artificial feed provided by farmers
q.m2.t3.020.d: Filtering phytoplankton dispersed by fish cages
q.m2.t3.021: What advantage does co-culture offer farmers regarding market risks?
q.m2.t3.021.a: Mitigating risks through diversified income from multiple species
q.m2.t3.021.b: Guaranteeing fixed prices for single-species products
q.m2.t3.021.c: Reducing labor costs by automating feeding processes
q.m2.t3.021.d: Allowing exclusive focus on high-demand species like fish
q.m2.t3.022: What critical factors must be assessed for successful nutrient removal in co-culture?
q.m2.t3.022.b: Proximity to urban settlements for market access
q.m2.t3.022.c: Historical use of the site for salt production
q.m2.t3.022.d: Uniform water depth across the farming area
q.m2.t3.023: Bivalves effectively remove dissolved inorganic nutrients like nitrate from water
topic.m2.t4.title: Integrated Multi-trophic Aquaculture technique in fish farming

q.m2.t4.040.a: Feeding levels in the food web
q.m2.t4.041: Sea cucumbers in IMTA systems help by:

q.m2.t4.047: Which of the following best describes IMTA’s main concept?
q.m2.t4.047.a: Ecological integration of multiple trophic levels

topic.m2.t5.title: Boosting the circular economy, farmed aquatic food bioeconomy and blue biotechnology
q.m2.t5.001: Which Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) is directly linked to ending hunger and improving aquaculture’s role in food security?
q.m2.t5.001.d: SDG 16: Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
q.m2.t5.002: What is the primary focus of the EU Circular Economy Action Plan regarding aquaculture?
q.m2.t5.002.a: Reducing waste and promoting resource efficiency
q.m2.t5.003: Integrated Multi-Trophic Aquaculture (IMTA) helps the circular economy by:
q.m2.t5.003.a: Recycling nutrients across species like fish, shellfish, and seaweed
q.m2.t5.003.c: Raising only one high-value fish species
q.m2.t5.004: Which tool is widely applied to evaluate the environmental impact of aquaculture systems?
q.m2.t5.005: What is a key barrier to circular aquaculture adoption in the EU?
q.m2.t5.005.d: Lack of global demand for seafood
q.m2.t5.006: Blue biotechnology in aquaculture is primarily focused on:
q.m2.t5.006.a: Developing innovative products and processes from aquatic organisms
q.m2.t5.007: A benefit of valorizing fish processing waste is:
q.m2.t5.007.a: Producing fishmeal, oils, or bioactive compounds
q.m2.t5.008: Why is aquaculture important for SDG 14: Life Below Water?
q.m2.t5.008.a: It reduces pressure on wild fish stocks by providing alternatives
q.m2.t5.008.d: It eliminates all marine species interactions
q.m2.t5.008.e: It bans aquaculture in coastal zones
q.m2.t5.009: What does the term 'Blue Economy' primarily emphasize?
q.m2.t5.009.a: Sustainable use of ocean resources for economic growth and jobs
q.m2.t5.010: Which aquaculture innovation reduces water discharge by recycling within the system?
q.m2.t5.011: A major advantage of IMTA in coastal areas is:
q.m2.t5.011.a: Mitigating eutrophication through nutrient uptake by seaweed and shellfish
q.m2.t5.012: What is one key environmental concern addressed by circular aquaculture?
q.m2.t5.013: Which European country is a leader in RAS trout production?
q.m2.t5.014: What is the role of aquaculture in the EU Bioeconomy Strategy?
q.m2.t5.014.a: To promote sustainable food systems and reduce reliance on imports
q.m2.t5.014.b: To focus exclusively on fossil fuel innovation
q.m2.t5.015: Why are mussel farms in Spain considered a case study for circular aquaculture?
q.m2.t5.015.a: They recycle nutrients naturally while producing high-quality seafood
q.m2.t5.015.b: They rely on heavy chemical treatments
q.m2.t5.016: What is a challenge identified in the report regarding public perception of aquaculture?
q.m2.t5.016.a: Concerns about sustainability and environmental impacts
q.m2.t5.016.b: Confidence that aquaculture has zero impacts
q.m2.t5.016.d: Assumptions that aquaculture only benefits developing nations
q.m2.t5.017: How does digitalization benefit sustainable aquaculture?
q.m2.t5.017.a: By improving monitoring, efficiency, and traceability
q.m2.t5.018: Which aquaculture practice aligns with circular economy by reducing reliance on fishmeal?
q.m2.t5.018.a: Using insect- and plant-based alternative feeds
q.m2.t5.018.b: Feeding fish with fossil fuel residues
q.m2.t5.018.e: Relying only on wild fish oil
q.m2.t5.019: According to the report, which global framework recognizes aquaculture’s role in climate change mitigation?
q.m2.t5.020: Why is Life Cycle Assessment critical in aquaculture innovation?
q.m2.t5.020.a: It identifies hotspots in energy, water, and feed use
q.m2.t5.021: What is a key advantage of recirculating aquaculture in arid regions?
q.m2.t5.021.a: Low water use and controlled conditions
q.m2.t5.022: Which aspect of aquaculture innovation directly supports SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production)?
q.m2.t5.022.a: Minimizing waste and promoting circular use of resources
q.m2.t5.023: How can seaweed contribute to climate change mitigation?
q.m2.t5.023.a: By absorbing CO₂ and providing biomass for sustainable products
q.m2.t5.024: What is one proposed roadmap action for boosting aquaculture sustainability?
q.m2.t5.024.a: Aligning policies and providing incentives for innovation
q.m2.t5.025: In circular aquaculture, waste-to-product strategies include:
q.m2.t5.025.a: Converting sludge into bioenergy or fertilizers
q.m2.t5.026: Which international body sets trade rules that impact aquaculture sustainability?
q.m2.t5.027: Why are eco-certifications important for aquaculture?
q.m2.t5.027.a: They build consumer trust and incentivize sustainability practices
q.m2.t5.028: What is a future direction highlighted for aquaculture resilience?
q.m2.t5.028.a: Adopting digital monitoring and carbon reduction strategies
q.m2.t5.029: What is the link between aquaculture and the EU Green Deal?
q.m2.t5.029.a: Aquaculture supports low-carbon, resource-efficient food systems
q.m2.t5.030: Which aquaculture species grouping best illustrates IMTA?
q.m2.t5.031: A major economic advantage of circular aquaculture is:
q.m2.t5.032: What role do consumers play in aquaculture’s circular economy?
q.m2.t5.032.a: Their preferences influence demand for sustainable seafood
q.m2.t5.033: What does 'waste valorization' mean in aquaculture?
q.m2.t5.033.a: Transforming waste into valuable products like biofertilizers or bioenergy
q.m2.t5.034: How can aquaculture support SDG 13 (Climate Action)?
q.m2.t5.034.a: By lowering emissions and adopting renewable energy
q.m2.t5.035: Which EU initiative fosters the bioeconomy, including aquaculture?
q.m2.t5.036: Why is consumer awareness critical to aquaculture sustainability?
q.m2.t5.036.a: It drives demand for certified, sustainable seafood
q.m2.t5.037: Which aquaculture technology can help integrate circularity into urban areas?
q.m2.t5.038: How can aquaculture contribute to the blue bioeconomy?
q.m2.t5.038.a: By producing sustainable biomass and bio-based products
q.m2.t5.038.d: By focusing solely on fossil resources
q.m2.t5.039: What is one governance challenge highlighted in the report?
q.m2.t5.039.a: Fragmented regulations across EU member states
q.m2.t5.040: What do Spanish mussel farms demonstrate about aquaculture?
q.m2.t5.040.a: That shellfish farming can provide ecosystem services while producing food
q.m2.t5.040.c: That circularity cannot exist in practice
q.m2.t5.040.d: That nutrient cycles must be ignored
q.m2.t5.040.e: That aquaculture is unsustainable by default
q.m2.t5.041: Which innovative feed ingredient reduces reliance on fishmeal?
q.m2.t5.042: In the context of aquaculture, the term 'circularity' refers to:
q.m2.t5.042.a: Closing resource loops by reusing and recycling inputs
q.m2.t5.043: How does the WTO affect aquaculture?
q.m2.t5.043.a: Through setting global trade rules that impact seafood markets
topic.m2.t6.title: Environmental footprints of aquaculture activities and products through a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)
q.m2.t6.001: What does the term environmental footprint in aquaculture describe?
q.m2.t6.001.a: The overall environmental impacts of a production activity, including resource use, emissions, pollution, and habitat alteration
q.m2.t6.001.b: Only the carbon footprint of aquaculture systems
q.m2.t6.001.c: The financial costs of aquaculture operations
q.m2.t6.001.d: The size of the farm cages in meters
q.m2.t6.002: Which aquaculture system is generally recognized as having the lowest carbon footprint?
q.m2.t6.002.a: Extensive shellfish farming (e.g., mussels, oysters, clams)
q.m2.t6.002.c: Offshore fish farming with imported feed
q.m2.t6.003: What is considered the main contributor to greenhouse gas emissions in intensive fish farming?
q.m2.t6.003.c: The color of nets and buoys
q.m2.t6.003.d: Methods applied to reduce stress of fish
q.m2.t6.004: What is the water footprint in aquaculture primarily used to assess?
q.m2.t6.004.a: Freshwater use and pollution across all production stages
q.m2.t6.004.b: The drinking water consumed by farm workers
q.m2.t6.004.c: The visual clarity of seawater in farms
q.m2.t6.004.d: How much rain falls on average in the farm area
q.m2.t6.005: Which component of water footprint refers to freshwater extracted directly from natural sources?
q.m2.t6.006: Which footprint component includes the rainwater embedded in feed crops like soy or corn?
q.m2.t6.007: What does the term grey water footprint represent?
q.m2.t6.007.a: The theoretical volume of water needed to dilute pollutants to safe levels
q.m2.t6.007.b: The volume of water consumed by workers at the farm
q.m2.t6.007.c: The seawater flowing through offshore cages
q.m2.t6.007.d: The amount of paint used for antifouling purposes
q.m2.t6.008: Which farming method usually has the lowest water consumption?
q.m2.t6.009: Which common practice helps reduce aquaculture’s water footprint?
q.m2.t6.009.a: Biofilters or wetlands to treat wastewater
q.m2.t6.009.d: Using renewable energy to power the farm
q.m2.t6.010: What is the main environmental concern linked to nutrient pollution in aquaculture?
q.m2.t6.010.b: Increasing the size of fish cages
q.m2.t6.011: Which of the following is a primary source of nutrient inputs in aquaculture systems?
q.m2.t6.012: What can high nutrient levels cause in aquaculture areas?
q.m2.t6.012.a: Reduced dissolved oxygen and increased fish stress
q.m2.t6.012.b: Increased predator density outside the farms
q.m2.t6.013: Which aquaculture system contributes most to eutrophication risk?
q.m2.t6.013.a: Intensive tank or marine cage systems
q.m2.t6.014: What is the main way Integrated Multi-Trophic Aquaculture (IMTA) reduces nutrient pollution?
q.m2.t6.014.a: Balancing species (fish, shellfish, algae) to reuse nutrients and waste
q.m2.t6.014.c: Releasing extra nutrients to fertilize wild plants
q.m2.t6.015: Which parameter is often used to measure nutrient impacts in aquaculture waters?
q.m2.t6.016: What is one major habitat impact of aquaculture cages on the seafloor?
q.m2.t6.017: Which sensitive habitat can be damaged by aquaculture structures in coastal areas?
q.m2.t6.018: Which practice helps reduce habitat conflicts in aquaculture?
q.m2.t6.019: What kind of assessment is often mandatory before building farms near protected areas?
q.m2.t6.020: Which type of system can cause land-use impacts even without direct marine effects?
q.m2.t6.021: What is the carbon footprint of extensive shellfish farming compared to other animal protein sources?
q.m2.t6.022: Which renewable energy source is often suggested for reducing aquaculture’s carbon footprint?
q.m2.t6.023: Which production stage often dominates mussel farming’s footprint?
q.m2.t6.023.a: Boat fuel use and plastic socks
q.m2.t6.024: Which aquaculture product does not require external feed?
q.m2.t6.025: Why are Life Cycle Assessments (LCA) important in aquaculture?
q.m2.t6.025.a: They identify critical impact stages across the production cycle
q.m2.t6.025.b: They increase the number of fish harvested automatically
q.m2.t6.026: Which stages does a cradle-to-grave LCA include?
q.m2.t6.026.a: From raw materials to end-of-life disposal
q.m2.t6.026.d: From final client back to farms
q.m2.t6.027: Which aquaculture system often has the highest energy demand?
q.m2.t6.028: What do sustainability certifications like ASC ensure?
q.m2.t6.028.a: Compliance with environmental and social standards
q.m2.t6.028.b: Higher farm profits regardless of impacts
q.m2.t6.028.c: That farms use the most advanced technology
q.m2.t6.029: What does the BAP certification evaluate?
q.m2.t6.029.a: Farms, hatcheries, feed mills, and processing plants
q.m2.t6.029.b: Taste and palatability of seafood products
q.m2.t6.029.d: The appeareance of final products on the shelf
q.m2.t6.030: Which aspect does eco-efficiency analysis combine?
q.m2.t6.030.c: The color of cage ropes and buoys
q.m2.t6.031: Which indicator is typical in eco-efficiency analysis?
q.m2.t6.031.a: Kg CO₂ per euro of product
q.m2.t6.031.d: Liters of water as input in aquaculture operations
q.m2.t6.032: What is a major challenge in applying LCA to aquaculture?
q.m2.t6.032.a: Data gaps and variability across farms
q.m2.t6.032.b: That fish cannot be measured in kilograms
q.m2.t6.032.c: That can be applied only in shrimp farming
q.m2.t6.033: Which positive effect can mussel farming have on water quality?
q.m2.t6.033.a: Nutrient removal and improved water clarity
q.m2.t6.034: What change is often seen in benthic fauna under offshore cages?
q.m2.t6.034.a: Increased opportunistic species due to organic enrichment
q.m2.t6.034.b: Complete disappearance of all marine life
